description_long: This object is a solar energy infrastructure installation featuring multiple rectangular solar panels arranged in a staggered, grid-like pattern. The panels are mounted on a flat, rectangular base with a visually striking black-and-white striped surface, interspersed with blue elements that may represent electrical or structural components. The rear of the installation is bordered by a tall, solid black vertical panel, possibly serving as a windbreak or structural support. The solar panels themselves are dark with a grid pattern, suggesting photovoltaic cells, and are supported by a series of angled mounts and frames. The overall design is modular, with clear separations between panel groups and visible support structures. The installation appears suitable for placement in urban environments such as rooftops, parking lots, or dedicated solar farms.
description: A modular solar energy installation with multiple grid-patterned panels on a striped base, bordered by a tall black rear panel and blue structural elements.
description_view_0: From this view, the staggered arrangement of the solar panels and the striped base are prominent, with the tall black rear panel visible on the right.
description_view_1: This side view shows the solid black rear panel dominating the profile, obscuring most of the solar panels and base details.
description_view_2: Here, the angled mounts and blue structural elements are more visible, along with the layered arrangement of the solar panels on the striped base.
description_view_3: This frontal view highlights the full width of the installation, the grid-like layout of the panels, and the tall black rear panel spanning the background.
attribute_panel_orientation: Panels are tilted at approximately 20-30 degrees, facing toward the open side opposite the tall black rear panel; orientation appears fixed.
attribute_mounting_structure_type: ground-mounted with visible angled aluminum or steel frames and a solid rear support panel.
attribute_array_configuration: Multiple panels arranged in a staggered, grid-like cluster, approximately 6-8 panels in total.
